disease.name: Respiratory Failure
disease.note: Respiratory failure is the inability of the respiratory system to maintain adequate gas exchange. Type 1 (hypoxemic): PaO2 <60 mmHg; Type 2 (hypercapnic): PaCO2 >45 mmHg with or without hypoxia. Management follows ARDSnet lung protective ventilation and evidence-based weaning protocols.

immediateDangerSigns.description: Fatigue, paradoxical breathing, inability to protect airway

historyFeatures.note: Late sign; central cyanosis indicates severe hypoxia
Hypercapnic encephalopathy: confusion, drowsiness, asterixis, coma
COPD, OHS, neuromuscular disease, chest wall deformity, CNS depression
Pneumonia, PE, ARDS, ILD, pulmonary oedema

examFeatures.note: Thoracoabdominal asynchrony indicates respiratory muscle fatigue
Sign of increased work of breathing

riskFactors.note: Most common cause of type 2 respiratory failure
GBS, myasthenia gravis, ALS, muscular dystrophy

subtypes.name: Type 1 Hypoxemic Respiratory Failure (PaO2 <60 mmHg)
subtypes.management: High-flow nasal oxygen (HFNC); treat underlying cause (pneumonia, PE, ARDS); lung protective ventilation if no improvement; prone positioning for ARDS

subtypes.name: Type 2 Hypercapnic Respiratory Failure (PaCO2 >45 mmHg)
subtypes.management: NIPPV/BiPAP first-line (if pH 7.25–7.35 and cooperative); treat precipitant; respiratory stimulants (doxapram) only if NIV not suitable; intubation if worsening acidosis, coma, exhaustion

subtypes.name: Acute Respiratory Distress Syndrome (ARDS)

keyFeatures.note: PCWP <18 or no clinical evidence of left atrial hypertension

subtypes.management: Lung protective ventilation (ARDSnet protocol: TV 6 mL/kg PBW, plateau pressure ≤30 cmH2O); high PEEP; prone positioning for PaO2/FiO2 <150; neuromuscular blockade (early); ECMO for refractory

bedside.name: Respiratory rate and pattern monitoring
bedside.indication: trend RR, accessory muscle use, paradoxical breathing

laboratory.name: Arterial blood gas (ABG)
laboratory.indication: define type of failure; guide ventilator settings; assess response to therapy
laboratory.note: Type 1: PaO2 <60, PaCO2 normal/low; Type 2: PaCO2 >45 with acidosis (acute) or normal pH (chronic compensated)

imaging.name: CT chest (non-contrast or contrast)
imaging.priority: if ARDS uncertain or underlying pathology unclear

advanced.name: Weaning parameters (RSBI, MIP, NIF, VT, VC)
advanced.note: RSBI <105 (rapid shallow breathing index) predicts successful weaning. MIP < -30 cmH2O good

advanced.name: Spontaneous breathing trial (SBT)
advanced.note: Performed on pressure support 5–8 cmH2O or T-piece for 30–120 min

management.protocolReference: ARDSnet Ventilation Protocol (2000); BTS Guidelines on NIV (2023); ATS/ERS Critical Care Consensus

pathways.criteria: mild hypoxia, no acidosis, no respiratory fatigue
treatment: Oxygen via nasal cannula or simple face mask to target SpO2 92–96%
Treat underlying cause (antibiotics, bronchodilators, diuretics, steroids)
Positioning: semi-recumbent, encourage coughing and deep breathing
Monitor with serial ABGs and pulse oximetry
Physiotherapy for airway clearance
pathways.escalation: If SpO2 cannot be maintained or PaCO2 rises, escalate to HFNC or NIV

pathways.severity: moderate (type 1 worsening or type 2 with pH >7.25)
pathways.criteria: PaO2/FiO2 100–200, or hypercapnia with pH 7.25–7.35 and cooperative patient
treatment: High-flow nasal oxygen (HFNC) up to 60 L/min, FiO2 0.4–1.0
OR NIPPV (BiPAP) for hypercapnic failure: IPAP 10–25 cmH2O, EPAP 4–8 cmH2O, titrated to target TV 6–8 mL/kg
NIV contraindications: coma, cardiovascular instability, facial deformity, unable to protect airway, vomiting
Lung protective ventilation if intubated: TV 6 mL/kg PBW, plateau pressure ≤30 cmH2O
Treat precipitant aggressively
pathways.reassessment: ABG in 1–2 hours; if pH <7.25 or worsening, consider intubation

pathways.criteria: PaO2/FiO2 <100 (severe ARDS), pH <7.25, exhaustion, coma, hemodynamic instability
treatment: Early intubation and invasive mechanical ventilation
Lung protective ventilation per ARDSnet protocol:
- TV 6 mL/kg predicted body weight (PBW: males 50+0.91×(height-152.4); females 45.5+0.91×(height-152.4))
- Plateau pressure ≤30 cmH2O
- PEEP: high PEEP strategy (PEEP/FiO2 table per ARDSnet)
- Respiratory rate to achieve pH 7.30–7.45 (max RR 35)
- Permissive hypercapnia if plateau pressure limits tidal volume
- I:E ratio 1:2–1:3 (inverse ratio if oxygenation refractory)
Prone positioning for PaO2/FiO2 <150 (12–16 hours/day)
Neuromuscular blockade (cisatracurium) if PaO2/FiO2 <120 + prone (early course 48 h)
ECMO (VV-ECMO) if PaO2/FiO2 <80 on FiO2 >0.9 or uncompensated hypercapnia despite optimal ventilation
Sedation: goal RASS -2 to -5 (light sedation unless severe ARDS)
Conservative fluid strategy (ARDSnet fluid protocol: target CVP <4 or PCWP <8 if not in shock)
Daily SBT/weaning assessment: RSBI <105, adequate cough, no vasopressors
pathways.escalation: ECMO referral criteria: PaO2/FiO2 <80 for >6 h or pH <7.25 with PaCO2 >60 for >6 h despite optimal settings

disease.emergencyPriority: HIGH - severe hypoxemia or hypercapnia with acidosis requires immediate respiratory support escalation
diagnosticClues: PaO2 <60 mmHg (type 1) or PaCO2 >45 mmHg (type 2) on ABG
tachypnoea with accessory muscle use and paradoxical breathing
failure to maintain SpO2 despite increasing oxygen
exclusionClues: normal ABG with adequate oxygenation excludes respiratory failure
hypoxia that corrects immediately with low-flow O2 suggests mild V/Q mismatch
isolated tachypnoea with normal ABG may be anxiety or compensation
rapid improvement with bronchodilators suggests asthma/COPD exacerbation, not fixed respiratory failure
